abstract: This study aimed to assess self-medication with antibiotics and evaluate knowledge and attitudes toward rational antibiotic use among women of reproductive age. This descriptive study was conducted among 1002 women who attended a state hospital in the southern region of Türkiye. Data were collected using a Personal Information Form, the Antibiotic Use Scale (AUS) and the Knowledge-Attitude Scale for Rational Antibiotic Use (RAUKAS). The mean AUS score was 40.4 ± 17.9, while the RAUKAS knowledge and attitude scores were 27.7 ± 8.9 and 30.6 ± 12.6, respectively. Overall, 24.5% of women reported self-medication with antibiotics within the last 6 months. The most common indications were cold or influenza (62.4%), toothache or dental swelling (58.0%), sore throat (51.8%), fever (31.4%), cough (25.3%), urinary burning (23.3%), headache (19.2%), abdominal pain (16.7%), fatigue (15.9%), and skin infection (11.8%). In addition, 30.0% kept antibiotics at home, 64.0% had never heard of antibiotic resistance, and attitude toward rational antibiotic use was the strongest predictor of self-medication ( < 0.001). Self-medication with antibiotics is practiced and represents a significant public health concern, with insufficient awareness of rational antibiotic use, highlighting the need for targeted educational interventions and public health strategies.

title: Management of Achromobacter xylosoxidans bacteraemia with an atypical antimicrobial resistance pattern
abstract: is an aerobic, Gram-negative bacillus typically associated with opportunistic infections. Bacteraemia caused by this organism is rare. We report a case ofbacteraemia in a young patient who presented with fever and a systemic inflammatory response and was successfully treated with targeted antimicrobial therapy, consisting of ciprofloxacin (a fluoroquinolone) and amikacin (an aminoglycoside), guided by antimicrobial susceptibility testing.

title: Resurgence of Typhoid Fever in Ghana: A Scoping Review of Epidemiology, Antimicrobial Resistance Patterns, Geographic Distribution, and Public Health Interventions
abstract: Typhoid fever remains a major public health threat in Ghana, where the intensification of a long-standing endemic challenge is driving a re-emergence of the disease. This resurgence is fuelled by a complex interplay of escalating antimicrobial resistance (AMR), inadequate water and sanitation (WASH) infrastructure, and persistent diagnostic limitations. This scoping review provides a comprehensive synthesis of the current evidence on the epidemiology, AMR patterns, diagnosis, treatment and public health interventions for typhoid fever in Ghana. A systematic scoping review of peer-reviewed literature, national surveillance data and public health reports from January 2010 to August 2025 was conducted using databases including PubMed, Scopus and institutional repositories. The incidence of typhoid fever remains high, with significant outbreaks in regions like Oti linked to environmental degradation from illegal mining. A critical shift in antimicrobial resistance is observed, with widespread resistance to first-line agents now compounded by emerging resistance to ciprofloxacin and decreased susceptibility to third-generation cephalosporins. Diagnostics remain a major challenge, with an over-reliance on the unreliable Widal test and underutilization of blood culture, which fuels a cycle of misdiagnosis and inappropriate antibiotic use. While public health interventions like WASH improvements and typhoid conjugate vaccine (TCV) rollouts are underway, they face significant logistical and financial hurdles to full implementation. The re-emergence of typhoid fever in Ghana is a complex public health crisis demanding an integrated response. Addressing this challenge requires strengthening diagnostic capacity, enforcing antimicrobial stewardship, accelerating TCV implementation, and making sustained investments in WASH infrastructure to break the cycle of transmission.

title: Clinical and microbiological evaluation of a phytotherapeutic intrauterine therapy in mares with bacterial endometritis: a preliminary study
abstract: Endometritis is a major cause of subfertility in mares and an important source of economic loss in the equine industry. Growing concerns regarding antimicrobial resistance have stimulated interest in alternative therapies. To evaluate the clinical efficacy of a phytotherapeutic intrauterine treatment in mares with endometritis and its in vitro antimicrobial activity against uterine isolates. Twenty mares with bacterial endometritis based on uterine culture, cytology, and ultrasonography were randomly assigned to two groups (n = 10). Control mares received uterine lavage followed by intrauterine infusion of lactated Ringer's solution, whereas treated mares received lavage followed by infusion of a phytotherapeutic solution. Clinical response was evaluated at 24, 48, and 72 h, and 10 days post-treatment using bacteriological culture, endometrial cytology, and ultrasonography. Treatment response was defined as negative uterine culture associated with negative endometrial cytology (<2 PMNs/field). No differences were observed between groups (P > 0.05) for bacterial clearance, cytological improvement, or treatment response. On Day 10, persistent bacterial growth was detected in 50% of control mares and 60% of treated mares. The most frequent isolates were Escherichia coli, Klebsiella spp., Streptococcus β-hemolyticus, and Staphylococcus spp. All isolates were classified as susceptible to gentamicin in vitro, whereas the phytotherapeutic solution showed no detectable antimicrobial activity. The small sample size and absence of an antimicrobial-treated comparison group may limit interpretation of the findings. Under the conditions of this preliminary study, the phytotherapeutic intrauterine treatment did not improve clinical or microbiological outcomes compared with uterine lavage alone.

Mechanistic insights into the inhibition of biofilm formation and antibacterial action of endophytic Streptomyces strain KJ7TB2 isolated from the Indian Sundarbans Mangrove estuary
abstract: To explore the mechanism involved in the inhibition of biofilm formation and antibacterial action of endophyticsp. isolated from the Indian Sundarbans Mangrove Forest. In this study,sp. KJ7TB2 was isolated from the Indian Sundarbans. An ethyl acetate extract was subjected for GC-MS profiling. The mechanisms of antibacterial and antibiofilm action were also explored.molecular docking and dynamics simulation analyses were performed. The GC-MS analysis of the extract revealed 14 distinct bioactive secondary metabolites. The extract exhibited potent MIC values of 250 μg/mL and 125 μg/mL againstMTCC 1195 andATCC 27853. Antibiofilm investigation further revealed inhibition of biofilm formation in both pathogens especially inMTCC 1195, likely by disrupting initial surface adhesion and exopolysaccharide synthesis. Also, the extract inhibited swarming motility, suggesting interference with flagella-driven motility and quorum sensing. The brine shrimp lethality assay confirmed a favorable safety profile, recording limited mortality (36.84% at 2×MIC over 24 h).molecular docking and dynamics simulation analyses exhibited robust affinity for two key biofilm-associated proteins, exhibiting significant binding energy (ΔG). The study highlightssp. KJ7TB2 as a potential source for the discovery of safe, potent therapeutics.

title: Nano-ferrous sulfide regulates conjugative transfer of antibiotic resistance genes: Synergistic effects of extracellular reactive oxygen species, Fe(2+), and mineral particles
abstract: Conjugative transfer of antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) in aquatic environments severely exacerbates global health risks. While iron (oxyhydr)oxide minerals are well known to regulate ARGs conjugative transfer in bacteria, the roles of reduced iron minerals remain poorly understood. Here we demonstrate that low concentrations (25-50 mg/L) of nano-FeS improve ARGs transfer efficiency by up to 707.12 %. Nano-FeS and its oxidation-derived species, including extracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), Fe, and mineral particles, trigger intracellular ROS production to initially strengthen bacterial adhesion. These factors further increase cell membrane permeability via physicochemical damage, thereby promoting plasmid transmembrane transfer. Meanwhile, moderate ROS accumulation also upregulates plasmid transport gene expression and provides metabolic energy for conjugation. Beyond these dominant physiological/biochemical responses, mineral particles bridge adjacent bacteria to facilitate intercellular contact. In contrast, high nano-FeS dosage (500 mg/L) reduces conjugation efficiency by 42.19 %-79.29 %. Nano-FeS and its oxidation-derived species impair cell adhesion and energy metabolism by inducing excessive intracellular ROS, and cause lethal membrane damage. Moreover, mineral particles exert a prominent coating effect that ultimately inhibits conjugative transfer. Notably, this bidirectional regulatory effect is consistently observed across various real aquatic matrices including paddy field pore water and pond bottom water. Our findings highlight that reduced iron minerals serve as redox-sensitive "switches" governing ARGs conjugation, providing new insights into iron chemistry for mitigating ARGs propagation in engineered and natural environments.

title: Diverse and ultraviolet-inducible phage-associated antibiotic resistance genes in wastewater treatment plants
abstract: Phage-mediated transduction is an underappreciated route of antibiotic resistance gene (ARG) dissemination in wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs), yet the diversity and fate of phage-associated ARGs remain poorly resolved. Here, a 5-year monthly metagenomic survey of 538 influent, activated sludge, and effluent samples from two municipal WWTPs in Nanjing, China, was combined with laboratory-scale UV dose-response experiments to profile the phage-encoded resistome and its fate along the treatment train. A total of 168 phage-associated ARG subtypes spanning 23 drug classes were recovered, approximately 1.7-fold more than catalogued for comparable environments in IMG/VR, with multidrug- and diaminopyrimidine-resistance genes dominating the catalogue and efflux pumps constituting the major resistance mechanism; 64.9 % of subtypes were WWTP-exclusive, highlighting pronounced habitat specificity. Caudoviricetes overwhelmingly carried the ARGs and were primarily putatively linked to Gammaproteobacteria, Betaproteobacteria, and Actinobacteria. Biological treatment markedly restructured the phage-associated resistome (PERMANOVA R = 0.19-0.34, p = 0.001), whereas conventional UV disinfection produced no significant bulk abundance reduction (p > 0.05). Dose-response experiments across 0-80 mJ/cmrevealed a biphasic pattern: low-to-moderate doses (10-20 mJ/cm) induced prophages and transiently elevated phage-fraction ARG concentrations by 0.3-0.8 logcopies/L (≈2- to 6-fold), whereas higher doses (≥40 mJ/cm) drove progressive inactivation. These findings expose a previously underappreciated paradoxical release of phage-associated ARGs within the operational UV window of Chinese municipal reactors and argue for coupling UV with complementary barriers to curb transduction-mediated resistance dissemination.

title: Population structure of the mitochondrial cytochrome b gene region and microbiological analysis of cyst fluids from Coenurus cerebralis and hydatid cysts obtained from sheep in the Southeastern Anatolia Region of Türkiye
abstract: Parasitic diseases are widely observed in different geographical and climatic regions and constitute significant problems in terms of animal health, public health, and livestock-based production systems. This study aimed to determine the molecular characterization and population genetic characteristics of Coenurus cerebralis and hydatid cyst cases observed in sheep in Siirt province, located in the Southeastern Anatolia Region of Türkiye, based on the mitochondrial cytochrome b (cytb) gene region, which was selected due to its ability to provide a balance between conserved structure and sufficient variability for reliable intraspecific discrimination, as well as to determine the presence of bacterial agents in cyst fluids and their antimicrobial susceptibility profiles. Between March and June 2025, 50 C. cerebralis and 50 hydatid cyst samples were collected from a licensed slaughterhouse. The cytb gene region was amplified by PCR from genomic DNA isolated from protoscoleces of hydatid cysts and scolices of C. cerebralis, and phylogenetic and population genetic analyses were performed following sequencing. In addition, bacterial agents were isolated from cyst fluids, and the antimicrobial susceptibilities of the isolates were evaluated. As a result of sequence analyses, reliable sequences of 538 bp in length were obtained in both parasite groups; six different haplotypes were identified in C. cerebralis isolates and eleven in hydatid cyst isolates. Population genetic analyses revealed moderate haplotype diversity (0.698 ± 0.050) and low nucleotide diversity (0.00258 ± 0.00041) with non-significant neutrality tests in the C. cerebralis population, whereas in the hydatid cyst population, both haplotype diversity (0.482 ± 0.087) and nucleotide diversity (0.00152 ± 0.00039) were low; however, significant negative Tajima's D and Fu's Fs values indicated a possible demographic expansion. Microbiological analyses revealed the presence of bacterial agents in 26% of C. cerebralis cysts and 6% of hydatid cysts. Antibiogram results showed that although most isolates were susceptible to some broad-spectrum antimicrobials, antimicrobial resistance was determined in varying levels. In conclusion, the findings shed light on the genetic structure and demographic dynamics of both parasites and demonstrate that cysts may pose a potential risk for secondary bacterial infections, highlighting the importance of antimicrobial susceptibility testing in treatment approaches.
